qs.q: Artificial intelligence can supply answers; only human beings can ask the right questions.

branches.q: A machine that thinks for us is useful; a machine that decides for us is dangerous.

qs.q: Peace is not the absence of war but the presence of justice.

branches.q: Strength deters aggression; wisdom prevents it. *(Operation Sindoor year — expect a strength/security-adjacent abstract prompt.)*

qs.q: Progress that costs the earth is not progress but borrowing.

branches.q: Nature does not negotiate; it only keeps accounts.

qs.q: What gets counted begins to count; what cannot be counted begins to disappear. *(Census/data/measurement year.)
Trust is the invisible infrastructure of a society.
The examined life is harder in an age when algorithms examine us first.
Courage is not the loudest voice in the room but the truest one.
A society is judged not by the heights of its towers but by the dignity of its margins.
Memory makes a nation as much as geography does. *(Heritage/anniversaries cycle.)
Discipline is remembering what you want most over what you want now.
The opposite of knowledge is not ignorance but certainty.
Freedom of expression is the foundation of harmony, not its enemy.
A developed nation is measured not by the size of its economy but by the dignity of its citizens. *(Viksit Bharat 2047.)
India must grow wise before it grows old. *(Demographic transition/dividend/ageing.)
The digital divide of the future will be of agency, not of access.
When women lead, the definition of power itself changes.
Federalism is a conversation, not a contract. *(Delimitation/16th FC/Governor friction year.)
An education that teaches answers but not questions prepares students for examinations, not for life.
In a world of walls, building bridges is an act of strategy as much as of faith. *(Multipolarity/strategic autonomy.)
The future of India's villages will decide the future of India's cities.
The energy transition is a moral transition before it is a technological one.
Justice delayed is legitimacy denied.
Cities are engines of growth only when they are also homes of equity.
Sport builds the character of individuals and the confidence of nations. *(Recurring UPSC filler genre.)

sections.t: A. Indian Culture — Art, Literature, Architecture

qs.q: The year 2026 marks the 500th anniversary of the establishment of the Mughal Empire. Discuss the Mughals' contribution to Indian art, architecture and composite cultural heritage, and assess their enduring impact on India's civilisational identity.

branches.q: Regional variations in Indo-Islamic architecture under the different Sultanates.
Creative synthesis in the Mughal and Rajput schools of miniature painting.

qs.q: "The recently inscribed Maratha Military Landscapes represent a unique synthesis of geo-strategic vision and architectural innovation." Discuss the salient features of Maratha hill-fort architecture.
Discuss the objectives and significance of the Gyan Bharatam Mission in reviving India's manuscript heritage, and examine its role in promoting the Indian Knowledge System (IKS).
"Continuous habitation sites offer a unique window into India's civilisational continuity." Discuss with reference to recent archaeological findings in India.
Trace the evolution of rock-cut cave architecture in India from the Mauryan to the Gupta period, with reference to the major cave complexes of western and eastern India.

branches.q: Salient features of Buddhist stupa architecture in India.

qs.q: Trace the evolution of the Nagara style of temple architecture from the Gupta period to the medieval period, highlighting continuities and changes.

branches.q: Vijayanagara temple architecture and cultural synthesis, with special reference to Hampi.

qs.q: Examine the contribution of the Ahom dynasty to the art, architecture and administrative culture of medieval Assam, with particular reference to the Moidams.
Discuss how Mauryan art and architecture, particularly the Ashokan pillars and edicts, reflected the ideals of statecraft, moral governance and the propagation of Dhamma.

The Bhakti and Sufi movements were powerful agents of social transformation and cultural synthesis, not merely religious phenomena. Discuss their role in bridging social divides and enriching India's composite culture.
Indian textile traditions have been a hallmark of civilisational continuity from the Indus Valley to the present. Discuss their evolution and significance in India's cultural heritage.

Indian literature, from Sangam poetry to modern regional writing, has been a powerful medium of social commentary. Discuss the evolution of India's literary traditions and their contribution to national consciousness.
Discuss the Bengal School's dialogue between indigenous tradition and Western influence in modern Indian art.

branches.q: Classical dance and music traditions as intangible cultural heritage — role of UNESCO's Representative List.

qs.q: Critically analyse how British economic policies transformed India's traditional production systems, disrupted indigenous industries and restructured the Indian economy to serve imperial interests.

branches.q: The 'Drain of Wealth' theory as an economic critique of colonialism and its role in the freedom movement.
Socio-economic impact of the commercialisation of agriculture in colonial India.

qs.q: "The Royal Indian Navy Mutiny and the INA trials created conditions that hastened the end of British rule." Discuss the significance of these events in the final phase of the freedom struggle. *(80th anniversary: 1946–2026.)

"Peasant and tribal uprisings in colonial India were as significant a part of the national movement as the organised political struggle." Discuss with suitable examples. *(Birsa Munda 150th anniversary anchor.)
Critically examine the methods, achievements and limitations of the 19th-century social reform movements and their continuing relevance.

branches.q: Contributions of women reformers and women's organisations from the colonial period to early independence; role of women across phases of the national movement.

qs.q: Compare and contrast the methods and contributions of the revolutionary nationalists with the Gandhian approach to India's independence.

branches.q: Revolutionary nationalism in Bengal and Punjab.

qs.q: Discuss how the Quit India Movement of 1942 marked a decisive shift in the freedom struggle, in mass participation and in the British reassessment of colonial rule.
Examine the role of the Indian press in shaping public opinion and mobilising the masses during the colonial period, with reference to key publications and press legislation.
Critically examine the causes of the Revolt of 1857 and assess why, despite its widespread nature, it failed to dislodge British rule.
"The Swadeshi Movement was as much an economic protest as a political one." Discuss in the context of the Partition of Bengal (1905).

"The States Reorganisation Act, 1956 was a landmark attempt to reconcile linguistic diversity with national unity." Discuss its process, challenges and enduring impact on Indian federalism. *(70th anniversary: 1956–2026.)

branches.q: Recent political-administrative reorganisation demands in North-East India (Frontier Nagaland Territorial Authority) in their historical context.

qs.q: Discuss the process and challenges involved in the integration of princely states — with special reference to Hyderabad and Junagadh — into the Indian Union. *(Sardar Patel 150th anniversary anchor.)

Evaluate the impact of land reforms in post-independence India on rural social structure, agricultural productivity and empowerment of marginalised communities. Why was their success uneven across states?
Trace the evolution of India's non-aligned foreign policy in the early post-independence years and assess its relevance in today's multipolar world.

"The American Revolution was not merely a colonial revolt but a defining moment in the global history of democratic ideas." Discuss its significance on the occasion of the 250th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence. *(4 July 2026.)
Discuss the internal and external factors that catalysed decolonisation in Asia and Africa after the Second World War, and evaluate how newly independent nations navigated state-building.

"The Cold War was not merely a military standoff but a contest of development models." Examine its impact on newly independent nations, particularly in shaping their economic strategies and foreign policies.
The 19th century saw nationalism unify Italy and Germany while disintegrating multi-ethnic empires. Discuss the contrasting outcomes of nationalist movements in 19th-century Europe.
"The post-World War I settlements redrew boundaries but sowed the seeds of future instability." Critically examine how Versailles shaped the 20th century's political trajectory.

branches.q: Rise of Fascism and Nazism between the wars and its consequences.
Post-WWII boundary redrawing and lasting geopolitical fault lines.

qs.q: Discuss the mechanism of the Indian monsoon system — the roles of the ITCZ, subtropical jet stream and Somali Current in its onset, progression and withdrawal — and the factors responsible for its inter-annual variability and their implications for agriculture.

branches.q: Mechanisms through which El Niño affects monsoon variability; cascading impact on agriculture and food security.
La Niña mechanism and implications for monsoon patterns and disaster risk in South Asia.
Indian Ocean Dipole influence on the monsoon and its importance for agricultural planning.

qs.q: Explain the mechanism of Western Disturbances and discuss their increasingly erratic behaviour in the context of global warming.

branches.q: What is an 'atmospheric river'? Its role in extreme rainfall events and the adequacy of India's early-warning systems.

qs.q: Account for the increasing seismic activity across northern India. Discuss the plate-tectonic setting of the subcontinent, the geological factors behind Himalayan seismic vulnerability, and India's seismic zonation.

"The North Indian Ocean witnessed one of its deadliest cyclone seasons in 2025." Discuss the factors behind the rising frequency and intensity of post-monsoon Bay of Bengal cyclones — including the SST linkage and why the Bay is more cyclone-prone than the Arabian Sea — and examine India's preparedness.

"Heatwaves are emerging as a 'silent disaster'." Discuss the geographical and anthropogenic factors behind their rising frequency and intensity in India, their cascading impacts, and the effectiveness of Heat Action Plans.

Himalayan glacial retreat is accelerating, with rising incidents of GLOFs and land subsidence in fragile mountain towns. Discuss the geographical causes of these phenomena and their socio-economic consequences for river-dependent populations. *(Dharali/Joshimath anchor.)
What are the causes and consequences of increasing landslide vulnerability in the Western Ghats? Discuss with reference to recent catastrophic events.
Examine the factors responsible for the increasing incidence of urban flooding in Indian cities, with special reference to changing rainfall patterns and unplanned urbanisation.
Nearly 40% of India's coastline is eroding. Analyse the physical and anthropogenic causes of coastal erosion and the implications for India's Blue Economy aspirations.

branches.q: Causes of seawater intrusion in coastal aquifers and remedial measures.

qs.q: "Critical minerals are the new frontier of global economic geopolitics." Discuss their global and Indian distribution, India's import-dependence vulnerabilities, and the strategic measures under the National Critical Minerals Mission.

"The suspension of a major trans-boundary water-sharing treaty has brought South Asian river-basin geography into sharp focus." Discuss the physiographic and hydrological characteristics of the Indus system and the implications of changed water-sharing arrangements.

branches.q: Contrast Himalayan and Peninsular river systems — geological origins, regimes, and water-resource implications.

qs.q: Discuss the major inter-state river water disputes and the geographical and political factors complicating their resolution.

branches.q: Feasibility and environmental implications of the Interlinking of Rivers programme.

qs.q: India extracts more groundwater than any other country, yet several basins approach water-scarce status. Discuss the geographical pattern of India's groundwater crisis and its impact on agriculture and drinking-water security.

"India's renewable energy ambitions are constrained as much by geography as by technology." Discuss the regional distribution of solar and wind potential and how renewables are reshaping India's economic geography and energy security.
Discuss the locational rationale behind India's major industrial corridors and their potential to reshape industrial geography, including emerging semiconductor and green-energy clusters.

branches.q: Factors influencing iron & steel industry location and the changing significance of raw-material linkages under globalisation.

qs.q: Discuss the strategic significance of polymetallic nodules in the Central Indian Ocean Basin under the Deep Ocean Mission, and evaluate the technological and environmental challenges of deep-sea mining.

branches.q: Why is the Arctic gaining geopolitical significance? India's interests in the Northern Sea Route and critical mineral deposits.

qs.q: Discuss the major soil types of India, their distribution, and the soil-degradation challenges threatening food security.

branches.q: The mismatch between cropping patterns and agro-climatic zones — geographical basis and climate-resilient planning.

qs.q: Discuss the major forest types of India (Champion & Seth classification) and the climatic, edaphic and anthropogenic factors determining their distribution. *(Never asked in 13 years — static safety net.)
Describe the major features of the ocean floor. How does India's extensive continental shelf influence its resource potential and maritime jurisdiction? *(Never asked — static safety net.)
Examine the factors responsible for the formation and global distribution of hot deserts.

branches.q: Global distribution of major fishing grounds and factors behind their productivity.
Coral reef formation and threats from climate change (ongoing global bleaching event).
Causes and consequences of desertification in India's arid and semi-arid regions.

qs.q: "Census 2027, currently underway, will for the first time since 1931 collect comprehensive caste data." Examine the significance of caste enumeration for evidence-based social policy, and discuss the concerns regarding its execution and use. *(Single biggest Society story of the cycle.)

"A country can be demographically young in aggregate and demographically old in its most developed states at the same time." Examine the policy dilemmas India's uneven fertility transition poses for delimitation, healthcare planning and labour-force strategy.

branches.q: Demographic dividend — the policy window and how to capitalise before it closes.
Challenges of India's growing elderly population and adequacy of social security.

qs.q: "Women's labour-force participation has risen, yet unpaid care work remains heavily skewed against them." Discuss this paradox with reference to recent official data.

branches.q: Factors behind India's low ranking on economic participation in global gender-gap indices.
Socio-cultural and institutional barriers to women's under-representation in leadership.

qs.q: Examine the debate surrounding the Uniform Civil Code and its implications for religious diversity and gender justice.

branches.q: Indian secularism ('principled distance') versus the Western separationist model, tested by recent legislative and judicial developments.

qs.q: "Reservation was conceived as a time-bound remedy, yet persists eight decades on." Critically examine, with reference to recent debates on sub-classification and the creamy layer within SC/ST reservation. *(Davinder Singh anchor.)
"Caste continues to be a salient social reality despite decades of modernisation." Discuss its persistence in contemporary social and political life.

branches.q: "Sanskritisation, Westernisation and now digitalisation have each reshaped social change in India." Discuss changing dynamics of social mobility.

qs.q: Discuss the changing nature of marriage and family in India in the context of the rising economic independence of women.

branches.q: Linkages between women's education, age at marriage and fertility decline.

qs.q: Examine the factors driving internal migration — including post-pandemic reverse migration and the rise of Tier-2 cities — and the implications for source regions, destination cities and urban planning.
"Communalism in India is as much a product of colonial policies as of contemporary politics." Critically examine.

branches.q: Significance of the Places of Worship Act, 1991 for India's secular fabric in light of recent litigation.

qs.q: Discuss the phenomenon of regionalism in India and examine whether it strengthens or weakens the federal fabric.
"PVTGs require a differentiated developmental approach distinct from Scheduled Tribes in general." Discuss the rationale and design of the PM-JANMAN Mission.

branches.q: Obstacles to implementing the Forest Rights Act two decades on.
Tension between development projects and habitat rights of indigenous communities (ecologically sensitive island territories).

qs.q: "Globalisation has had a differential impact on rural and urban India." Discuss with examples from agriculture, employment and consumption.

branches.q: Impact of social media and digital platforms on traditional social structures and interpersonal relationships.
Evolving social impact of diaspora linkages — remittances, reverse migration, return entrepreneurship.

qs.q: "AI is reshaping the nature of work in India, but its benefits are not gender-neutral." Discuss the social implications of AI-driven change, with particular reference to the gender digital divide.

"Malnutrition and anaemia persist as India's silent emergencies despite growth and food-security legislation." Suggest measures to break the intergenerational cycle of malnutrition. *(Also GS-2 #47 angle — revise once.)

branches.q: Effectiveness of the PC&PNDT Act and Beti Bachao Beti Padhao against the skewed child sex ratio.

qs.q: "Smart Cities have not adequately addressed the needs of the urban poor." Critically examine the inclusiveness of India's urban development model, with reference to housing, sanitation and services for slum populations.

The Supreme Court's advisory opinion in the 16th Presidential Reference held that gubernatorial discretion under Articles 200–201 is not justiciable, yet "prolonged, unexplained and indefinite inaction" invites limited judicial scrutiny. Examine the constitutional balance struck between the Governor's discretion and the legislative supremacy of States. *(Biggest constitutional development of the cycle — cross-confirmed in every source.)

branches.q: Why was "deemed assent" rejected as "a takeover of the role of a separate constitutional authority"? Implications for future Centre–State legislative deadlocks.
Scope and limits of the Governor's discretionary powers in light of recent controversies.

qs.q: Examine the shift from "entitlement-based" to "compliance-driven" fiscal federalism in the recommendations of the 16th Finance Commission. How does growing reliance on cesses and surcharges affect the constitutional scheme of devolution under Article 270?

branches.q: Composition and functions of the Finance Commission under Article 280; do horizontal devolution criteria reconcile equity with efficiency?
branches.label: static

qs.q: "The 16th Finance Commission has shifted local-body grants from an entitlement to a conditionality-based framework." Discuss the implications of entry-level conditions (SFC constitution, audited accounts) for the goals of the 73rd and 74th Amendments.

branches.q: "The State Finance Commission remains the weakest constitutional link in fiscal decentralisation." Examine.
"Functions, Functionaries and Funds have given way to the challenge of Functionality." Assess.

qs.q: Examine the GST Council as an exercise in cooperative federalism under Article 279A. In light of disputes over compensation and rate rationalisation ("GST 2.0"), is Indian fiscal federalism trending towards centralisation?

"Fiscal transfers have received more attention than the institutions of inter-governmental consultation." Evaluate the role of the Inter-State Council, Zonal Councils and allied mechanisms in resolving Centre–State friction.

The proposed delimitation of Lok Sabha constituencies on updated population data raises concerns about the north–south balance of representation. Examine the constitutional provisions governing delimitation (84th Amendment freeze, Article 82) and the implications for Indian federalism.

branches.q: The Article 368 special-majority-plus-ratification route for amendments touching federal balance.

qs.q: The 50th anniversary of the 1975 Emergency has revived debates on safeguards against misuse of emergency powers. Discuss the 44th Amendment reforms and evaluate whether they suffice to prevent recurrence.
Examine the constitutional provisions of the 106th Amendment (women's reservation) and discuss the challenges — census, delimitation, intra-party barriers — in its effective implementation.

Trace the evolution of the Basic Structure Doctrine from Shankari Prasad to Kesavananda Bharati and beyond, and discuss its role in preserving constitutional supremacy while permitting democratic change. *(Never asked standalone 2013–25 — static safety net.)

branches.q: "Constitutional morality becomes meaningful only when it constrains everyday state action." Comment.

qs.q: Examine the constitutional, federal and practical challenges of implementing 'One Nation, One Election', in light of the 129th Amendment Bill and Joint Parliamentary Committee deliberations.

The Anti-Defection Law continues to face criticism over its effectiveness and the Speaker's role. Critically examine the functioning of the Tenth Schedule in light of recent judicial pronouncements on delayed adjudication. *(Never asked standalone — flagged across all sources.)

"Parliamentary Committees are a vital instrument of accountability, yet their recommendations remain non-binding." Examine the committee system's role in executive oversight, with special reference to the Financial Committees.

branches.q: Declining sitting days and rising resort to ordinances — causes, consequences, remedies.

qs.q: Has the Rajya Sabha effectively functioned as a council of states? Examine its constitutional role and special powers.

branches.q: Parliamentary privileges and their relationship with fundamental rights.
Leader of the Opposition — constitutional significance and statutory framework.

qs.q: Critically examine the evolution, achievements and limits of Public Interest Litigation in India — is judicial legislation through PIL antithetical to separation of powers or indispensable given executive inertia?

branches.q: Article 142 — "complete justice" or judicial overreach?

qs.q: Would a codified memorandum of procedure compromise judicial independence? Discuss the debate on transparency in the collegium system of judicial appointments.
Examine the constitutional basis and continuing challenges of the administrative tribunal system in India.
Compare the mechanisms of judicial review in India and the United Kingdom. How does a written constitution with a codified basic-structure doctrine depart from parliamentary sovereignty?

branches.q: India's Westminster inheritance and its distinct federal departures.

qs.q: The DPDP Act, 2023 seeks to operationalise the Puttaswamy right to privacy. Examine whether it adequately balances privacy with national security and digital governance — and how the amendment of RTI Section 8(1)(j) reshapes the transparency–privacy boundary. *(Rules operationalised this cycle.)
Examine the evolving free-speech jurisprudence under Article 19(1)(a) against the challenges of social media and digital platforms.

branches.q: Sedition — Section 124A IPC to Section 152 BNS: continuity or change?

qs.q: "The horizons of Article 21 continue to expand through judicial interpretation." Illustrate with recent rulings extending protections to non-traditional claims of dignity and access.

branches.q: Preventive detention's compatibility with Article 21; custodial violence safeguards.

qs.q: Discuss the doctrine of harmonious construction between Fundamental Rights and Directive Principles. Does the current framework achieve social justice without compromising liberty? *(Static safety net.)

branches.q: Article 32 vs Article 226 writ jurisdictions — compare and contrast.

qs.q: Discuss the constitutional vision of secularism as 'principled distance'. How is it tested by recent legislative and judicial developments concerning religious endowments?

branches.q: The Essential Religious Practices doctrine and its critics.

qs.q: Discuss the constitutional and statutory basis of the Election Commission's power to conduct Special Intensive Revision of electoral rolls (Article 324, Section 21 RPA 1950). Does this power admit judicially enforceable limits?

branches.q: Why is the right to vote a constitutional/statutory rather than fundamental right? Safeguards against arbitrary exclusion.

qs.q: Does the Selection Committee composition under the CEC and Other ECs Act, 2023 adequately safeguard the independence of the Election Commission envisaged by Article 324?
Discuss the CAG's powers and functions as 'guardian of the public purse' and the challenges to its effectiveness and independence in the era of PPPs and off-budget financing.
"Recommendatory bodies without teeth?" Evaluate the design and performance of the Lokpal and the NHRC as accountability institutions.
Compare the Fifth and Sixth Schedule frameworks for tribal areas, examine their effectiveness, and suggest reforms for tribal self-governance. *(Static safety net.)
Discuss the constitutional and statutory provisions governing Indian citizenship and the challenges posed by recent amendments.

branches.q: The Article 370 abrogation and statehood-restoration debate — implications for federal structure.
Section 17A Prevention of Corruption Act — executive accountability vs protection from vexatious prosecution.

qs.q: With the four Labour Codes brought into force, examine their potential to balance ease of doing business with the social security of the workforce. *(Nov 2025 trigger — also GS-3 lens; revise once.)

Digital Public Infrastructure has transformed e-governance from service delivery into a foundational layer of economy and society. Examine how India's DPI model advances inclusion, and the exclusion risks it must guard against. *(E-governance is UPSC's highest-frequency governance theme.)
The deployment of AI in public administration promises efficiency but raises opacity, bias and diffused accountability. Discuss the safeguards necessary for responsible AI in governance.

Twenty years after enactment, evaluate the RTI Act's achievements and limitations, and discuss how the transparency-privacy tension introduced by the data-protection framework can be reconciled without diluting the citizen's right to know.
Assess the role of technology-driven grievance-redressal mechanisms (CPGRAMS, AI-enabled interfaces) in bridging the citizen–State trust deficit.
Mission Karmayogi seeks to move the civil services from a rule-based to a role-based framework. Can capacity-building address the structural rigidities of Indian bureaucracy?

branches.q: Lateral entry — domain expertise vs the generalist tradition; can they coexist?

qs.q: Discuss the key recommendations for police reform and why implementation has remained elusive despite judicial and executive directives.
The proliferation of sector regulators has not been matched by functional autonomy. In the context of the new data-protection architecture, discuss the challenges of regulatory independence.

SHGs have evolved from thrift-and-credit collectives into agents of rural transformation (10 crore women, 91 lakh SHGs, Lakhpati Didi). Evaluate their role in women's economic empowerment and the constraints on scaling.

Civil society occupies a contested space between partnership with and scrutiny by the State. Critically examine whether the FCRA-centred regulatory framework strikes the right balance between accountability and autonomy.
"Genuine decentralisation requires devolution of functions, finances and functionaries." Critically examine the extent to which PRIs have been empowered as institutions of self-government.

branches.q: India's experience with decentralised planning — bridging intent and ground outcomes.

qs.q: In the context of the Smart Cities Mission's conclusion, discuss the reforms needed to give Urban Local Bodies functional autonomy, financial resources and professional capacity.

The recognition of gig and platform workers under the Code on Social Security, 2020 remains largely unrealised. Discuss the challenges of extending meaningful social security to India's platform workforce. *(Never asked — high competitive edge.)
With health assurance extended to all citizens aged 70+, assess the preparedness of India's public health system for an ageing population, and the adequacy of the elderly-welfare policy framework.
Examine the effectiveness of government interventions for tribal welfare with special reference to PVTGs. *(Cross-ref GS-1 #61.)

Evaluate the effectiveness of the NFSA and PDS in ensuring food and nutrition security, and suggest reforms in targeting and delivery — including the case for shifting from calorie to nutrition security.

branches.q: POSHAN Abhiyaan and Mission Saksham Anganwadi against the malnutrition paradox.

qs.q: "Accessibility is not charity but enforceable right." In light of recent Supreme Court pronouncements, examine the implementation gaps in the RPwD Act, 2016 and the way forward. *(Cross-confirmed in all sources.)
The multiplicity of commissions for vulnerable sections raises concerns of overlapping mandates and limited effectiveness. Critically examine whether these bodies have secured substantive justice.
Strengthening pandemic preparedness requires an integrated One Health approach. Discuss the institutional reforms needed for a resilient public health system.

branches.q: Ayushman Bharat and the road to universal health coverage — systemic reforms needed.
Barriers to mental healthcare access and the Mental Healthcare Act, 2017.

qs.q: Five years into implementation, critically evaluate NEP 2020's progress in integrating education, skilling and employability for the demographic dividend.

branches.q: RTE — universal enrolment vs learning outcomes.

qs.q: India has recorded a substantial decline in multidimensional poverty. Critically examine whether these gains reflect genuine well-being improvements, and how the intersection of caste, gender and geography continues to shape deprivation.
Critically evaluate the impact of JAM/DBT on leakage reduction and last-mile welfare delivery, and the exclusion challenges that persist.

"In an era of transactional partnerships and weaponised market access, strategic autonomy is an operational necessity, not a Cold War inheritance." Critically examine India's multi-alignment doctrine in light of recent developments. *(The year's defining meta-theme — prepare as your strongest answer.)
"The 'Neighbourhood First' policy is being stress-tested by political churn along India's periphery." Critically examine the challenges to India's neighbourhood diplomacy and suggest a way forward.
Discuss the factors that have strained India–Bangladesh relations following the political upheaval in Dhaka, and the measures India can adopt to stabilise the relationship.

In the context of the May 2025 military escalation (Operation Sindoor), discuss the structural impediments to India–Pakistan normalisation and evaluate India's options for managing the adversarial relationship.

branches.q: India's principled opposition to third-party mediation — bilateralism under stress.
The new counter-terrorism doctrine's implications for neighbourhood diplomacy.

qs.q: Discuss the challenges India faces in maintaining influence in the Indian Ocean Region vis-à-vis Sri Lanka and the Maldives, amid domestic politics and Chinese inroads.

branches.q: HADR as an instrument of India's maritime soft power.
India's evolving maritime strategy against growing external presence in the IOR.

qs.q: Discuss the impact of the Myanmar civil war on India's border security and the options available to India.
"External pressure has quietly narrowed the trust deficit between New Delhi and Beijing, even as structural rivalry endures." Discuss the drivers and limits of the recent India–China stabilisation.
Discuss how India should navigate the India–Russia relationship amid intensifying Western sanctions pressure and Russia's deepening alignment with China.

The India–US partnership has faced unprecedented strain from trade disputes, divergent positions on Russia, and transactional American foreign policy. Critically analyse whether the partnership can withstand these pressures.

branches.q: Reconciling deepening ties with Washington with strategic autonomy after the 2026 trade understanding.

qs.q: "Trade tariffs have evolved from economic instruments into geopolitical weapons." Analyse how protectionism and tariff wars are reshaping global trade architecture, and India's strategy for a fragmented trading environment.

As Chair of an expanded BRICS in 2026, India seeks to "reform, not replace" the multilateral order. Discuss how India can leverage its chairmanship for the Global South without positioning BRICS as an anti-Western bloc. *(India hosts the 2026 summit — near-certain.)

branches.q: De-dollarisation and settlement in national currencies — opportunities and risks for India.

qs.q: "The Quad faces a credibility test at a moment of uncertainty in US commitments." Evaluate its relevance and trajectory for India's Indo-Pacific strategy.
Critically assess the SCO's importance for India given the membership overlap with adversarial states.
Examine the strategic drivers of India–EU convergence, culminating in the free trade agreement, and its implications for India's diversification.

branches.q: FTAs with developed economies as instruments of economic security and strategic diversification.

qs.q: Assess the prospects of the India–Middle East–Europe Economic Corridor (IMEC) amid geopolitical instability, comparing its strategic logic with existing alternatives.
"Energy security and diaspora welfare tie India's national interest inextricably to West Asia." Examine the implications of renewed regional conflict for India's strategic and economic interests.

branches.q: India's Gaza position — foreign-policy principles under stress.

qs.q: Discuss India's evolving position in climate negotiations and the effectiveness of its climate diplomacy, balancing development with commitments.
"Governance of frontier technologies is a new arena of diplomacy." Examine India's role in shaping global norms on AI and critical technologies. *(AI Impact Summit 2026 host.)
"The Indian diaspora is increasingly a strategic asset." Discuss its economic and diplomatic significance and the State's responsibility for its welfare abroad.
"Reformed multilateralism is impossible without a reformed UNSC." Critically examine the impediments to UNSC reform and India's case for permanent membership.

branches.q: The WTO's existential crisis — dispute-settlement paralysis, protectionism — and India's stake in rules-based trade.
Effectiveness of international counter-terrorism cooperation and the case for the CCIT.
India–Africa: from solidarity to strategic partnership; Act East/ASEAN in its sixth decade.
branches.label: T3

title: GS-3 Economy, Agriculture, S&T, Environment, DM & Security

qs.q: The 2026 India–US trade understanding reduced tariffs but required concessions on energy sourcing and market access. Critically examine the opportunities and risks bilateral trade deals pose for India's strategic autonomy and export competitiveness in an era of reciprocal tariffs.

The rationalisation of GST into a two-slab structure ("GST 2.0") is the most significant indirect-tax reform since 2017. Evaluate its likely impact on consumption, revenue buoyancy of Centre and states, and MSME compliance.

Examine the key recommendations of the 16th Finance Commission (2026–31), with special reference to changes in horizontal devolution criteria. Do they balance equity with efficiency among states? *(Also GS-2 lens — revise once, write to either directive.)

India has shifted from annual fiscal-deficit targeting to a debt-to-GDP ratio as the medium-term fiscal anchor. Discuss the rationale and the implications for transparency and counter-cyclical policy.

"India's challenge is no longer the quantity of jobs but their quality." Examine in the light of PLFS data, rising informality and the Employment-Linked Incentive scheme; suggest measures for productive formal employment.

branches.q: The four Labour Codes in force — flexibility vs worker welfare; state-level implementation challenges.

qs.q: High GDP growth with widening inequality undermines inclusive growth. Critically examine India's record using the Inequality-adjusted HDI and multidimensional poverty, and suggest correctives. *(Inclusive-growth cycle: asked 2016, 2017, 2019, 2020, 2022, 2024, 2025 — near-annual.)
Retail inflation fell to historic lows in 2025–26. How much credit does flexible inflation targeting deserve? Discuss the issues in the ongoing monetary-policy framework review.

The Economic Survey 2025–26 urges a shift from import substitution to scale, competitiveness and global-value-chain integration. Evaluate PLI and Make in India in this light and suggest next-generation manufacturing reforms.

branches.q: MSME constraints — credit, scale, formalisation — and recent measures including revised classification.

qs.q: Discuss the economic significance of India's DPI (UPI, ONDC, Account Aggregator) as a global template, and the accompanying challenges of digital exclusion, platform concentration and data security.

India's banking sector has moved from a "twin balance-sheet problem" to a "twin balance-sheet advantage". Examine the turnaround and the emerging risks — unsecured retail credit, deposit-growth slowdown.
Public capital expenditure has driven post-pandemic growth. Evaluate the sustainability of the capex-led model, why private investment has not revived commensurately, and how to crowd it in.
Discuss the significance of PM Gati Shakti and the National Logistics Policy in cutting logistics costs, and how high-speed rail corridors and new national waterways fit India's multimodal strategy.

branches.q: Inland waterways and regional air connectivity (UDAN) in reducing regional disparities.

qs.q: India's services exports, led by Global Capability Centres, anchor external-sector stability. Examine the opportunities and vulnerabilities of services-led growth in the age of AI.
India requires ~8% sustained growth for Viksit Bharat 2047. Identify the binding constraints — savings/investment, productivity, human capital — and the reforms to raise potential growth.

Why is PPP considered essential for India's infrastructure ambitions? Evaluate the National Monetisation Pipeline experience and suggest ways to make PPP contracts balanced and bankable. *(PPP cycle: 2017, 2020, 2022 — due.)
The new Income-tax Act, effective April 2026, replaces the 1961 law. Discuss how simplification can improve compliance and widen the base; why does the direct-to-indirect tax ratio remain a concern?

The proliferation of unconditional cash-transfer schemes by states raises concerns of "fiscal populism" crowding out capex. Critically examine, in the context of state fiscal health and welfare needs.

Cities are engines of growth, yet Indian urbanisation is constrained by unclear land titles, weak municipal finances and inadequate transit. Discuss, referencing recent Economic Survey recommendations.
A "China+1" realignment offers India a strategic export window. Examine the constraints — logistics, trade finance, standards, FTA utilisation — to becoming a major exporting nation.

branches.q: Why do FDI inflows remain below potential despite strong fundamentals?
Sources of external-sector risk and the toolkit for capital-flow volatility.

qs.q: The base-year revision of India's GDP series has renewed measurement debates. Discuss the significance of updating the base year and the challenges of capturing the informal and digital economy.

branches.q: Bringing the care economy into the monetised economy — policy interventions required.
Macroeconomic implications of India's pivot away from discounted Russian crude.

qs.q: Despite being the largest producer, India remains a large importer of pulses. Discuss the objectives and design of the National Pulses Mission and the agronomic, price-incentive and procurement reforms needed for self-sufficiency.

Opening Indian agriculture to subsidised imports was the most sensitive issue in recent trade negotiations. Examine the impact of agricultural trade liberalisation on farm incomes and food security, and how India can defend its policy space at the WTO and in bilateral deals.

branches.q: Legalising MSP — feasibility, implications, and WTO constraints on price support and public stockholding.

qs.q: Explain the factors driving the shift towards high-value and horticultural crops. How can diversification be accelerated without compromising cereal security? *(Diversification cycle: 2017, 2021, 2023, 2025 — due.)

From AgriStack and the Digital Agriculture Mission to AI-advisories (Bharat-VISTAAR), technology is reshaping farming. Examine how digital public infrastructure in agriculture can improve productivity, credit and market linkage, along with the data-privacy concerns.

With 2025 witnessing weather disruption on 330+ days, climate volatility is the central risk to Indian agriculture. Discuss climate-proofing strategies — resilient seeds, insurance reform, adaptive cropping calendars.

branches.q: Crop insurance alone cannot manage rising agricultural risk — the case for an integrated risk-management framework.

qs.q: Discuss the potential of natural farming to cut input costs and restore soil health, and the challenges of scaling the National Mission on Natural Farming without yield loss.

branches.q: Integrated Farming Systems — pathway from input-intensive production to income and ecological resilience.

qs.q: Examine how digitisation of land records (DILRMP, SVAMITVA), tenancy reform and land leasing can unlock agricultural investment and credit, drawing lessons from earlier land-reform phases. *(Land-reform cycle: 2016, 2021, 2023, 2024 — recurring favourite.)

Food processing is the critical link between farm incomes and non-farm employment. Examine its scope, bottlenecks, and the effectiveness of PLI-food processing and PM Kisan Sampada. *(Strongest single cycle in GS-3: 2017, 2019, 2020, 2022, 2025.)

With free food-grain distribution extended under PMGKAY, critically examine the fiscal and nutritional questions facing the PDS–procurement–buffer-stock architecture, and the case for moving from calorie security to nutrition security.

Groundwater depletion and inefficient irrigation are the twin water challenges of Indian agriculture. Evaluate micro-irrigation, PMKSY and reservoir rejuvenation, and suggest water-pricing and power-subsidy reforms.
"Sahkar se Samriddhi": examine the role of the revitalised cooperative sector — including the world's largest decentralised grain-storage plan — and FPOs in strengthening smallholder agriculture.

branches.q: Agricultural marketing reform via FPOs, digital markets and warehousing — expanding farmer choice while correcting bargaining power.

qs.q: Examine the potential of drones and precision agriculture (Namo Drone Didi) for rural livelihoods and women's empowerment.

branches.q: Livestock, dairy and fisheries as engines of rural income diversification.
Fertiliser-subsidy reform — nano-fertilisers, PM-PRANAM, balanced nutrient use.
Impact of export restrictions on farmer incomes and India's reliability as an agri-exporter.

qs.q: India hosted the AI Impact Summit 2026 (New Delhi Declaration). Discuss India's "application-led" AI development under the IndiaAI Mission, and how to balance productivity gains against risks to employment, privacy and information integrity.

branches.q: "Sovereign AI" requires more than indigenous LLMs — what should constitute India's AI-sovereignty framework?

qs.q: With the first Made-in-India chips rolling out and ISM 2.0 targeting equipment manufacturing and indigenous IP, examine the progress, persisting challenges and strategic significance of India's semiconductor ambitions. *(Asked 2025 — but the ISM 2.0/first-fab milestone justifies a re-angled return; prepare the update layer.)

The Gaganyaan programme and the proposed Bharatiya Antariksha Station mark India's transition to human spaceflight. Discuss their scientific, technological and strategic significance and the execution challenges.

branches.q: Space-sector reforms (IN-SPACe, SpaDeX docking) and growth prospects of India's private space economy.

qs.q: The Nuclear Energy Mission targets 100 GW by 2047, with SMRs and amendments to the Atomic Energy Act and civil-liability law enabling private participation. Critically examine the promise and the concerns.
What are quantum computing, communication and sensing? Discuss the National Quantum Mission's objectives and the strategic implications for cryptography and national security.
From BioE3 to Biopharma SHAKTI, India is positioning biotechnology as a growth driver. Examine how biomanufacturing can serve health security, agriculture and clean energy.

branches.q: Indigenous CAR-T therapy and gene-therapy trials — significance and the affordability challenge.

qs.q: Operation Sindoor demonstrated the decisive role of indigenous air-defence networks, drones and precision systems. Examine the significance of defence-technology indigenisation and Mission Sudarshan Chakra for India's deterrence posture.

branches.q: Atmanirbharta in defence — record production/exports and the reforms (R&D, private participation, iDEX) to sustain it.

qs.q: Satellite-broadband constellations, NavIC and the earth-observation build-up have made space applications central to connectivity and governance. Discuss the opportunities and regulatory challenges of the satcom revolution.

branches.q: How earth observation (NISAR) strengthens disaster management, agriculture and climate monitoring.

qs.q: Critical minerals are the new currency of technological power. Examine India's supply-chain vulnerabilities and evaluate the National Critical Mineral Mission and overseas acquisition efforts. *(Cross-ref GS-1 #40 — revise once.)

India has released genome-edited rice varieties, a first among major economies. Distinguish genome editing from transgenic GM technology and discuss the potential and regulatory concerns for agriculture.
Despite top-tier patent filing and publications, India's R&D spend remains under 1% of GDP. Examine how the ANRF and deep-tech startup policies can transform the research ecosystem.
Antimicrobial resistance is the "silent pandemic". Discuss its drivers across human health, animal husbandry and environment, and the One Health approach to contain it.

branches.q: Grid-scale energy storage and the battery value chain — from PLI cell manufacturing to recycling.
Deep Ocean Mission and Samudrayaan — blue-economy and strategic dimensions.

qs.q: Describe the major outcomes of COP30 at Belém — the Global Mutirão decision, tripled adaptation finance, the just-transition mechanism. Critically examine India's negotiating position on climate finance and the absence of a fossil-fuel roadmap.

India achieved 50% non-fossil installed capacity five years ahead of its NDC target. Examine the gap between capacity share and actual generation, and the grid-integration, storage and financing challenges of deepening the transition.

branches.q: System integration — grids, storage, green hydrogen — as the real frontier of the renewables transition.

qs.q: India's Carbon Credit Trading Scheme has entered its compliance phase with GHG-intensity targets. Explain the architecture of the Indian carbon market and its potential, with CCUS incentives, to decarbonise hard-to-abate sectors.
India's updated pledges rest on a 2070 net-zero target and LiFE. Examine the sectoral pathways and finance requirements of the low-carbon transition and the equity arguments India advances.

Despite NCAP and graded response mechanisms, North Indian winter air remains hazardous. Analyse why policy has delivered limited results and make the case for airshed-based, multi-sectoral air-quality management.
With the global plastics treaty stalling, national action matters more. Examine the effectiveness of India's single-use plastic ban and Extended Producer Responsibility in curbing plastic pollution.

branches.q: Circular economy — e-waste and battery-waste EPR regimes, recycling infrastructure, urban mining.

qs.q: The Forest (Conservation) Amendment and infrastructure in ecologically sensitive regions have intensified the conservation–development debate. Critically examine how India can reconcile strategic imperatives with forest conservation and community rights.

branches.q: Drivers of human–wildlife conflict (declining elephant census) and measures for coexistence.

qs.q: India has Asia's largest Ramsar network, yet urban wetlands degrade. Explain wetland ecosystem services and evaluate the conservation framework — 'wise use', Amrit Dharohar.

branches.q: Wetlands and mangroves as disaster-risk-reducing "development infrastructure" — and the limits of nature-based solutions.

qs.q: Recurring disasters from Joshimath to Dharali underline Himalayan fragility. Discuss carrying capacity for Himalayan towns and the reform of development planning in the region.

branches.q: Marine heatwaves and coral bleaching — impacts on coastal ecosystems and fisheries resilience.

qs.q: Environmental regulation in India is criticised simultaneously for obstruction and dilution. Examine the role of EIA, the NGT and citizen action in balancing development with protection.

branches.q: Why does river pollution persist despite Namami Gange? Governance reforms required.

qs.q: The Dharali (Uttarkashi) disaster of August 2025 exemplified cascading Himalayan multi-hazards — cloudbursts, debris flows, glacier-linked processes. Analyse the causes and the early-warning, zonation and regulation measures needed. *(GLOF mechanism: cross-ref GS-1 #36.)

The Disaster Management (Amendment) Act, 2025 is the first major overhaul since 2005. Discuss its key provisions — Urban Disaster Management Authorities, the disaster database — and whether it addresses the gaps revealed by recent disasters.

From Punjab's basin-wide floods to metropolitan inundation, 2025 underscored flood vulnerability. Discuss structural and non-structural measures for urban flood management and the integration of flood-risk planning into urban development.
Examine the adequacy of Heat Action Plans and discuss the case for notifying heatwaves as a disaster under the DM framework.

Disaster-risk financing is shifting from relief-centric allocation to risk reduction and insurance. Examine this transition in light of the 16th Finance Commission's DM grants and instruments like parametric insurance and anticipatory action.
Large parts of India including the NCR lie in high seismic-risk zones with poor structural compliance. Discuss measures to improve urban earthquake preparedness.

branches.q: Community-based DRR, local governments and Aapda Mitra in achieving Sendai targets.
Industrial/chemical disasters as failures of prevention and regulatory governance.

qs.q: Operation Sindoor signalled a doctrinal shift — treating cross-border terror attacks as acts of war warranting conventional response. Examine the strategic implications of this deterrence posture and its escalation risks.

With near-elimination of Left-Wing Extremism declared by the 2026 deadline, the challenge shifts from operations to consolidation. Examine the factors behind LWE's decline and the governance, development and rehabilitation strategy to prevent resurgence. *(LWE cycle: 2018, 2020, 2022, 2025 + the deadline itself.)

Drones have become the defining threat vector — from cross-border smuggling to swarm attacks. Examine the dimensions of the drone threat and evaluate the counter-UAS architecture, including Mission Sudarshan Chakra.

The Red Fort car blast of November 2025 exposed "white-collar" terror modules of radicalised professionals. Analyse the changing profile of terrorism in India and the intelligence, policing and counter-radicalisation responses it demands.

Organised cyber-fraud — "digital arrest" scams, transnational scam-centre networks — has become a national-security-scale problem. Examine the evolving threat and evaluate the institutional response, including I4C.

branches.q: Critical Information Infrastructure protection — framework and gaps.

qs.q: Examine India's border-management strategy towards China and Pakistan — smart fencing, Vibrant Villages, border-area development — against infrastructure asymmetry, infiltration and smuggling.

branches.q: Neighbourhood instability (Bangladesh, Myanmar, Nepal) as a vector for external state and non-state threats.

qs.q: India's maritime security environment is shaped by China's naval expansion, threats to undersea infrastructure and attacks on shipping. Discuss the challenges and evaluate responses including SAGAR/MAHASAGAR and coastal-security architecture.

Terror financing has migrated to cryptocurrencies, trade-based laundering and narcotics. Examine the evolving methods and the effectiveness of India's counter-measures — FATF processes, UAPA, financial intelligence.

branches.q: Virtual assets, offshore gaming and hawala-fintech hybrids — challenges to the AML regime.

qs.q: The information domain emerged as a full theatre of conflict during Operation Sindoor, with AI-generated disinformation at scale. Examine the threat of information warfare to internal security and democratic processes, and suggest a counter-disinformation strategy.

branches.q: Hybrid warfare — cyber, information, economic and kinetic instruments — and a whole-of-nation response.

qs.q: Analyse the roots of the Manipur conflict, its security implications — the Kuki–Meitei divide, Myanmar spillovers — and the elements of durable peace in the North-East.
The new criminal laws mandate technology-driven investigation. Examine how NATGRID, AI-based policing and forensic infrastructure can transform policing, and the safeguards needed against misuse.

The narcotics–terrorism–organised-crime nexus, fed by the Golden Crescent/Triangle and enabled by drones and darknet markets, is a multi-dimensional threat. Examine its dimensions and suggest a coordinated national response.

branches.q: Linkages between terrorists and organised crime at national and transnational levels.

sections.t: Section A(i) — Ethics and Human Interface

qs.q: "Law is the codified minimum of ethics; ethics is the uncodified maximum of law." Distinguish between law, morality and ethics as sources of guidance in public life, with examples of where they diverge.

What do you understand by the 'dimensions' of ethics? Explain how a single administrative decision — for example, deploying an AI system for welfare targeting — simultaneously raises descriptive, normative and applied ethical questions.

Sardar Patel's approach to the integration of states is often described as a triumph of firmness tempered by fairness. What lessons in statecraft, persuasion and institutional ethics does his life offer to today's civil servant? *(Patel 150th anniversary.)

branches.q: Ethical lessons from Birsa Munda's resistance (150th) / Gandhi's talisman applied to a current policy choice.

qs.q: "The family is the first school of values, but social media has become the second." Examine the changing role of family, society and educational institutions in inculcating values in the digital age.
Differentiate ethics of private and public relationships. Why does public office demand a stricter standard than private life, and where should the boundary of scrutiny lie?

"Consequences matter, but so do duties and character." Compare consequentialist, deontological and virtue-ethics approaches through the dilemma of a District Magistrate ordering an evacuation that will destroy livelihoods to save lives.

sections.t: Section A(ii) — Attitude, Aptitude, Emotional Intelligence

qs.q: "In an age of algorithmic echo chambers, attitude formation has been outsourced to the feed." Discuss the social-influence and persuasion functions of attitude, and how a public servant can counter misinformation-driven attitudes without coercion.
Distinguish between attitude and aptitude. Why do foundational values — integrity, impartiality, empathy — matter more than intelligence for the civil services?
"Artificial intelligence can replicate analysis but not empathy." Examine the role of emotional intelligence in administration in an era when routine decisions are increasingly automated.
How does emotional intelligence help in managing an ethnically polarised district following communal violence? Illustrate the specific EI competencies involved.

"Political attitude and bureaucratic attitude often collide in a democracy." How should a civil servant's attitude towards political executives be shaped by constitutional values rather than personal ideology?

sections.t: Section A(iii) — Thinkers and Moral Philosophy

qs.q: Kautilya holds that "in the happiness of his subjects lies the king's happiness." Examine the relevance of this dictum — and the Arthashastra's code for officials — to contemporary ideas of accountable administration.
Gandhi's doctrine of trusteeship is sometimes called utopian and sometimes prophetic. Evaluate its relevance to contemporary debates on billionaire wealth, CSR and ESG.

Rawls asks us to design society from behind a "veil of ignorance". Apply this device to one current Indian policy question (delimitation, reservation sub-classification, or climate burden-sharing) and state what it would demand.
"The Buddha's madhyama pratipada (middle path) and Aristotle's golden mean converge on moderation as a moral method." Discuss, with administrative applications.

branches.q: Thiruvalluvar on rectitude in public office; Confucius on rectification of names — relevance to codes of conduct.

sections.t: Section A(iv) — Public Service Values & Ethics in Public Administration

qs.q: "Neutrality is loyalty to the Constitution, not indifference to values." Examine the distinction between political neutrality and value-neutrality for the civil servant, in the context of a polarised information environment.

"The public servant's anonymity is dissolving in the age of the personal brand." Examine the ethical issues raised by civil servants' social-media visibility, and where legitimate outreach ends and self-promotion begins.
What is a conflict of interest? Illustrate with examples from public procurement, post-retirement appointments and regulatory revolving doors, and suggest institutional remedies.

"Accountability for an algorithm's decision cannot be delegated to the algorithm." Discuss the problem of diffused responsibility in AI-assisted administrative decisions and the safeguards ethical governance requires.

Laws, rules and regulations bind the civil servant; conscience guides. What should an officer do when a lawful order is unethical? Distinguish dissent, disobedience and whistleblowing as graded responses.

"Between the ethics of conviction and the ethics of responsibility, the statesman must choose responsibility." Examine this Weberian tension with reference to India's doctrine of calibrated military response to terrorism. *(Ethics of deterrence — Sindoor year.)

"Climate negotiations are a contest between historical responsibility and present capability." Examine the ethical principles — equity, CBDR, intergenerational justice — underlying India's climate diplomacy. *(Ethics in international relations slot.)

branches.q: Ethics of international funding — conditionality, sovereignty and the aid relationship.

qs.q: Corporate governance failures in India increasingly originate in founder-dominated startups rather than old conglomerates. Discuss the ethical deficits involved and the role of boards, auditors and independent directors.

"Information is the currency of trust between citizen and state." Discuss the philosophical basis of transparency, and how the RTI–data-protection tension should be resolved when the two rights collide in a specific case.
Distinguish a Code of Conduct from a Code of Ethics. Why has India adopted the former but never the latter for its civil services, and what would a meaningful Code of Ethics add?
"Corruption is not a transaction but an ecosystem." Analyse the institutional, social and psychological dimensions of corruption in India, and why enforcement-centric strategies alone fail.

"Data collected from citizens is held in trust, not owned by the state." Examine the probity obligations — purpose limitation, security, non-discrimination — that large state data exercises (Census 2027, welfare databases) impose on administrators.

"The utilisation of public funds is the first test of probity." Examine the ethical dimensions of competitive welfare populism ('freebies') financed by public borrowing, balancing welfare obligations against intergenerational equity.
Citizen's Charters promised a service revolution but lapsed into ritualism. What ethical work culture and service-delivery reforms would restore their promise?
Whistle-blowing sets conscience against loyalty. Evaluate the adequacy of India's whistle-blower protection framework and the ethical duties of the organisation towards the honest dissenter.

"Work culture is the aggregate of small ethical choices." Discuss how leadership behaviour, incentives and peer norms shape ethical climate in a government office, and the levers available to reform it.
Social audit converts beneficiaries into auditors. Examine its value as an instrument of probity and the conditions for its effectiveness.
"The true test of a civilisation is not the census, nor the size of cities, but the kind of man the country turns out." — Emerson. *(Census year resonance.)
"Real knowledge is to know the extent of one's ignorance." — Confucius. *(AI/expertise humility.)
"It is not power that corrupts but fear — fear of losing power." — Aung San Suu Kyi.
"The world has enough for everyone's need, but not for everyone's greed." — Gandhi. *(Climate/consumption.)
"Never do anything against conscience even if the state demands it." — Einstein.
"Justice will not be served until those who are unaffected are as outraged as those who are." — Benjamin Franklin.

As District Collector you deployed an AI tool to de-duplicate welfare rolls. It has cut leakage 30%, but a field visit reveals it is systematically excluding elderly widows without biometric-linked records; the vendor calls the error rate "within tolerance"; the state government is showcasing your district as a model. Identify the stakeholders and ethical issues; evaluate your options; state your course of action.
qs.title: AI welfare exclusion

qs.q: You are DM of a Himalayan district. IMD issues a probabilistic cloudburst warning during peak pilgrimage season. Full evacuation will destroy the season's livelihood for thousands and may prove unnecessary; not evacuating risks a Dharali-style catastrophe. The local MLA demands the yatra continue; your junior engineer reports illegal riverbed construction sheltering pilgrims. Discuss the ethical trade-offs and your decision framework.
qs.title: Disaster warning dilemma

qs.q: As Executive Engineer you discover falsified quality certificates in a national-highway flagship project shortly before its high-profile inauguration. Your superior advises silence — "rectification can follow the ribbon-cutting" — and hints your empanelment is due. Options, evaluation, action.
qs.title: Whistleblowing on a flagship project

qs.q: You head a district cyber cell. To crack a "digital arrest" fraud ring victimising hundreds of pensioners, your team proposes bulk access to call and payment records of thousands of citizens, exceeding the legal warrant's scope. The SP backs it as "the only way". Examine the competing values and lay out your response.
qs.title: Data privacy vs investigation

qs.q: A morphed video is inflaming tensions in your sub-division on the eve of a festival. Arresting the local youth who first shared it may trigger the very riot you fear; not acting emboldens organised amplifiers; a blanket internet shutdown will hurt exam-going students, gig workers and hospitals. As SDM, what will you do and why?
qs.title: Communal rumour on social media

qs.q: You are Secretary of a department finalising a large solar tender. Your spouse's consulting firm has, unknown to you until now, advised one bidder. Disclosure may derail a time-critical national project and your career; silence is likely to remain undetected. Identify the ethical issues and the correct sequence of actions.
qs.title: Civil servant's spouse and conflict of interest

qs.q: As Census charge officer you find enumerators in one tehsil informally sharing caste-column data with local political workers. Exposure will invite transfer-season retribution and delay the operation; silence corrodes the exercise's public trust. Course of action?
qs.title: Caste-data confidentiality

qs.q: As Finance Secretary of a fiscally stressed state, you are directed weeks before elections to release an unbudgeted cash-transfer scheme by diverting funds earmarked for school infrastructure. The CM's office cites popular mandate; the model code has not yet kicked in. Evaluate your options.
qs.title: Freebies before elections

qs.q: A woman contractual employee accuses your most efficient and well-connected tehsildar of sustained harassment. She wants action but fears losing her job; witnesses are silent; the ICC is headed by the accused's batchmate. As Collector, what do you do?
qs.title: Subordinate harassment complaint

qs.q: A mining lease promising 4,000 jobs in your aspirational district overlaps a PVTG habitat with unsettled Forest Rights Act claims. The Gram Sabha's consent is being managed through inducements by the proponent. As Collector chairing the FRA committee, weigh the interests and state your approach.
qs.title: Development vs PVTG habitat

qs.q: Your college friend, now a struggling journalist, asks you (a PIO) to leak a draft inquiry report implicating a minister, arguing the public interest. The report may change after due process; leaking violates rules; the final version may be diluted. What do you do?
qs.title: Old friend seeking favour

qs.q: As head of an overstretched disaster-response unit, your best officer shows signs of severe burnout and makes a serious operational error you can quietly cover. Reporting it triggers mandatory suspension during flood season; concealing it falsifies records. Discuss the ethical dimensions — compassion, accountability, institutional integrity — and your action.
qs.title: Officer burnout and team ethics

title: Public Administration Paper I — Administrative Theory

qs.q: "The identity crisis of Public Administration has yielded to an era of plural paradigms rather than a single dominant paradigm." Comment.

branches.q: "Each 'identity crisis' has enriched rather than enfeebled the discipline." Comment.
Locus-focus: "each answer has redrawn the boundaries of the discipline." Comment.

qs.q: "Wilson founded the discipline upon a dichotomy that his own later career as an administrator belied." Examine the continuing significance of Wilson's vision.

branches.q: The dichotomy as a claim for professional competence rather than a factual separation.

qs.q: "The New Public Administration's concern for relevance, values and social equity remains unfinished business for contemporary governance." Discuss with reference to the Minnowbrook perspective (I to III).

branches.q: NPA made social equity an administrative criterion, not merely a political aspiration.

qs.q: "New Public Management promised efficiency but delivered fragmentation; New Public Governance seeks to restore coherence through collaboration." Critically examine the paradigm shift.

branches.q: "NPG does not replace NPM; it corrects NPM's incomplete theory of the state."

qs.q: "Governance is government plus networks, markets and communities." Critically examine the implications of this reconceptualisation for the role and capacity of the state.
"Paul Appleby held that government is different in kind, not merely in degree, from private management." Critically examine the distinctiveness of public administration.

branches.q: "Publicness, rather than ownership, now defines the domain of Public Administration."

qs.q: "The New Public Service restores the citizen, not the customer, to the centre of administration." Comment.

branches.q: "Good governance is as much about legitimacy of process as efficiency of outcome."

qs.q: "Public Choice illuminates government failure, but cannot by itself guide the digital welfare state." Analyse.
"POSDCORB tells us what a chief executive does, but not what administration is for." Comment on the adequacy of Gulick's formulation.

branches.q: Urwick's classical principles and the 'proverbs' critique.
"Taylor and Fayol are complementary architects of organisational rationality" / "Fayol's principles have weathered time better than Taylor's techniques."

qs.q: "Scientific Management dehumanised the worker in the name of efficiency, yet its logic survives in every performance dashboard of the digital state." Examine.

"Weber's prophecy of the 'iron cage' has come true not through the failure of bureaucracy but through its success." Examine the continuing relevance of the ideal type and the post-Weberian developments culminating in the Neo-Weberian State.

branches.q: "Weberian bureaucracy remains resilient because post-bureaucratic reforms modify rather than abolish hierarchy, expertise and rules."
"The AI-enabled state requires a Neo-Weberian bureaucracy rather than a post-bureaucratic state."
"Weber's bureaucracy is an ideal type, not an empirical description of reality."
branches.label: short

qs.q: "For Chester Barnard, authority lies not in the person who commands, but in the acceptance of the person commanded." Explain.

branches.q: The 'zone of indifference' — why authority ultimately flows upward; why formal orders fail at the frontline.

qs.q: "Mary Parker Follett's 'law of the situation' anticipated the logic of contemporary collaborative governance." Discuss.

branches.q: 'Constructive conflict' as a design principle for inter-organisational governance.

qs.q: "The Hawthorne experiments discovered the 'social man' but ignored the structure of power within the organisation." Critically examine.

branches.q: "The Human Relations School humanised the workplace but romanticised the worker."

qs.q: "There is a fundamental incongruence between the needs of a mature personality and the demands of a formal organisation." Discuss Argyris' proposition and evaluate participative management as its remedy.
"Likert's 'System-4' organisation, held together by 'linking pins', makes participation the very technology of organisational integration." Examine.
"The classical and the human-relations schools are not rival theories but two halves of a single theory of organisation." Discuss.
"Simon's attack on the 'proverbs of administration' becomes more relevant, not less, in an AI-assisted administrative state." Explain.

McGregor's Theory X and Theory Y are less descriptions of workers than of managers' assumptions about workers. Examine their relevance to public-sector work culture. *(Gap-filler: McGregor untested in corpus.)

"Artificial intelligence offers a paradoxical answer to Simon's 'bounded rationality': machines extend the limits of rationality even as they narrow the space for human judgement." Examine the implications for administrative decision-making.

branches.q: "Bounded rationality is a more honest account of administrative choice than the myth of the 'economic man'."
branches.label: short

branches.q: Programmed-to-non-programmed decision continuum and how technology reshapes both ends.

qs.q: "The study of leadership moved from the traits of the leader, through the behaviour of the leader, to the demands of the situation." Examine, with reference to the Managerial Grid, Fiedler's contingency model and Hersey–Blanchard.

branches.q: "Leadership in public organisations is increasingly transformational rather than transactional."

qs.q: "Maslow's need hierarchy is more a philosophy of human nature than a verifiable theory of motivation." Examine.

branches.q: "Herzberg's hygiene factors only prevent dissatisfaction; they do not motivate."

qs.q: "Vroom's Expectancy theory explains why identical incentives motivate different employees differently." Examine its utility for public organisations.

branches.q: McClelland — the need for achievement rather than the lure of money distinguishes the high performer.

qs.q: "Public-service motivation is not a substitute for fair rewards, supportive working conditions and organisational justice." Discuss in the light of content, process and contemporary theories of motivation.
"In the digital workplace, the classical problem of communication has been inverted: from scarcity of information to its overload." Discuss the consequences for organisational communication.

branches.q: "Communication is the nervous system of an organisation, and the grapevine its informal pulse."
branches.label: short

qs.q: "Morale and motivation are related but distinct; high morale does not guarantee high productivity." Discuss.
Chris Argyris' single-loop and double-loop learning as a diagnosis of why government organisations repeat their failures. Comment. *(Gap-filler static.)
"Contingency theory rejects one best organisational structure, yet public organisations cannot escape constitutional and legal constraints." Discuss.
